title: What is Frequency in an Image?
concept: In an image, 'frequency' describes how quickly brightness changes as you move across pixels — high frequency means rapid, busy changes like fine texture, while low frequency means slow, smooth changes like open sky.

exercises.prompt: Recap: Why does a brick wall photo have a 'busier' brightness signal than a plain wall photo?

options.text: Its brightness bounces up and down rapidly as you scan across bricks and mortar lines
It has a larger file size

exercises.explain: Right — a busy, rapidly-bouncing brightness signal is exactly what we're about to give a name: frequency.

cards.title: Guitar strings, thick and thin
cards.body: Pluck a thick bass string and it vibrates slowly, producing a low rumble. Pluck a thin string and it vibrates rapidly, producing a high, bright note. That same 'how fast does it wiggle' idea is exactly what frequency measures — in sound, or in an image.

cards.title: What is frequency in an image?
cards.body: Frequency describes how quickly brightness changes as you move across an image. High frequency means brightness flips rapidly pixel to pixel (fine detail, texture, sharp edges). Low frequency means brightness changes slowly and smoothly (open sky, a plain wall, a soft gradient).

cards.title: A calm lake vs. choppy water
cards.body: A calm lake's surface rises and falls slowly and gently — low frequency. Choppy water in a storm bounces up and down rapidly, wave after wave in quick succession — high frequency. Scan across a smooth sunset sky and you get the calm lake; scan across a gravel driveway and you get the choppy water.

cards.title: Sweater texture vs. a clear sky
cards.body: Photograph a knit sweater and scan across it: the brightness zig-zags rapidly between yarn threads and shadows, thread after thread — high frequency. Photograph a clear blue sky and scan across it: the brightness barely changes at all from one edge to the other — low frequency.

cards.title: High frequency doesn't mean 'zoomed in'
cards.body: It's tempting to think high frequency just means 'zoomed in close.' Really it just means 'changes fast across space' — a wide shot of a gravel parking lot is still high frequency because the brightness still flips rapidly pixel to pixel, even though nothing is zoomed in.

cards.title: Low frequency vs. high frequency
cards.body: Two ends of the same scale:

left.text:
Slow, smooth brightness changes
Skies, plain walls, soft gradients

right.text:
Rapid, busy brightness changes
Texture, fine detail, sharp edges

exercises.prompt: Which of these would have the HIGHEST frequency content?

options.text: A close-up photo of coarse sand
A photo of a clear, cloudless sky
A photo of a smooth painted wall
A photo of an out-of-focus blurry background

exercises.explain: Correct — coarse sand's brightness flips rapidly grain to grain, which is exactly what high frequency means.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Low-frequency content means the brightness in an image changes slowly and smoothly as you scan across it.
exercises.explain: True. Low frequency is exactly this: gradual, gentle brightness change with no rapid jumps.

exercises.sentence: Frequency in an image describes how ___ brightness changes as you move across the pixels.
exercises.explain: Quickly is correct — frequency is all about the speed of brightness change across space, not color or overall brightness.

exercises.prompt: Which photo would produce a mostly LOW-frequency brightness signal?

options.text: A photo of a plain, cloudless sunset sky
A photo of gravel on a driveway
A photo of a wire fence

exercises.explain: Right — a plain sky's brightness barely changes at all across the frame, the definition of low frequency.

exercises.prompt: A photographer takes two shots of the same scene: one in sharp focus, one heavily out of focus (blurred). Which shot has more high-frequency content, and why?

options.text: The sharp shot — fine detail and crisp edges create rapid brightness changes that blurring smooths away
The blurry shot — blur adds extra texture
They're identical because blur only changes color, not brightness
Neither has any frequency content since nothing in the photo is moving

exercises.explain: Exactly! Blurring wipes out fast, high-frequency wiggles and leaves mostly the slow, low-frequency information behind. Next: there's a classic technique for splitting an image apart into these frequency components — let's meet it, purely as an idea.
